---
title: "Prompting"
subtitle: "A working vocabulary and a set of reliable patterns"
---

::: {.callout-note}
## Learning objectives
- Use the core prompting vocabulary: system prompt, user prompt, few-shot, chain-of-thought, role prompting.
- Choose a prompting pattern that matches the task.
- Iterate on prompts systematically instead of randomly.
:::

## Anatomy of a prompt

A conversation with an LLM is made up of three conceptually distinct message types:

- **System prompt** — set once per session, before any user input. Contains durable instructions: persona, output format, access rules, standing context (a document, a data schema, lab conventions). In raw API calls you control this directly; in wrapped products (ChatGPT, Claude.ai) it is partially or fully hidden.
- **User prompt** — the specific request for this turn. What most people mean when they say "the prompt."
- **Assistant turns** — the model's prior responses, fed back into context verbatim. In few-shot prompting, you pre-populate assistant turns with worked examples to pattern-match against.

```{mermaid}
%%| fig-cap: "The three message types all enter the model as one flat token stream."
flowchart LR
    subgraph ctx["Context window — one flat token stream"]
        direction TB
        S["**System prompt**\ndurable instructions, persona,\nformat rules, standing context"]
        U["**User turn**\nthis request"]
        A["**Assistant turn(s)**\nprior responses;\npre-populated for few-shot"]
    end
    ctx --> M(["LLM"])
    M --> O["Next-token\ncompletion"]
```

The model sees all three as a flat token sequence — there is no architecturally privileged "ground truth" channel. A well-structured system prompt shapes behavior because the model learned, during training, that system-prompt-style text predicts certain response patterns. It does not "obey" the system prompt; it continues a pattern it has seen before.

## Patterns that work

1. **Role + task + context + format** — "You are X. Do Y. Given Z. Return in format F."
2. **Few-shot** — show 2–3 input/output pairs before the real input.
3. **Decomposition** — explicitly break a complex task into subtasks.
4. **Self-critique** — ask the model to critique its own output before finalizing.
5. **Constrained output** — specify the exact schema (JSON, table columns, section headings).

## Patterns that reliably fail

- "Don't hallucinate." (It will. Better: provide sources.)
- "Be accurate." (Meaningless on its own.)
- "Summarize this paper" on a paper the model has never seen. (Use RAG or paste the paper.)

## Worked example: extracting structured info from a methods section

Suppose you want to pull the sequencing platform, read length, and library prep kit from a batch of methods sections.

**Weak prompt:**

> Summarize this methods section.

Output: a paragraph. Readable; useless for downstream data entry.

**Slightly better, still weak:**

> Extract the key sequencing parameters from this methods section.

Output: inconsistent — sometimes a list, sometimes prose, sometimes includes parameters you didn't want, sometimes omits ones you did.

**Strong prompt:**

```
You are a precise scientific data extractor.
Extract exactly the following fields from the methods section I provide.
Return a JSON object with these keys and no others:

- "platform": sequencing platform and model (string, or null if not stated)
- "read_length": read length in base pairs (integer, or null if not stated)
- "library_kit": library preparation kit name (string, or null if not stated)

If a field is not stated in the text, set it to null. Do not infer.
Do not add keys beyond the three listed.
Return only the JSON object — no prose, no explanation.

Methods section:
<text>
{{PASTE_METHODS_HERE}}
</text>
```

Why this works:

- Role framing sets the expectation for precision over fluency.
- Explicit field list prevents the model from deciding what counts as "key."
- `null` for missing data prevents fabrication of unstated values.
- Structural schema (key names, types, allowed values) beats prose description.
- "Return only the JSON object" suppresses verbose preamble.

**Iteration log** — when building an extractor for more than ten documents, keep a running table: prompt version, test input, output, verdict. Change one variable per iteration. Save the winner as a reusable template.

## Iterating on prompts

Treat prompting like any other optimization:

- Hold the test input fixed.
- Change one thing per iteration.
- Keep a log — prompt, output, verdict.
- Save the winner as a reusable template.

## Further reading

- Anthropic. (2025). [Prompt engineering overview](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/build-with-claude/prompt-engineering/overview). *Anthropic developer docs* — current reference for Claude-specific prompting; the principles generalize.
- Wei, J., et al. (2022). [Chain-of-thought prompting elicits reasoning in large language models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2201.11903). *NeurIPS 2022* — the paper that established chain-of-thought as a reliable technique.
- Brown, T., et al. (2020). [Language models are few-shot learners](https://arxiv.org/abs/2005.14165). *NeurIPS 2020* — the GPT-3 paper; Section 2.3 introduces few-shot prompting as used in practice.
- Schulhoff, S., et al. (2024). [The prompt report: A systematic survey of prompting techniques](https://arxiv.org/abs/2406.06608). arXiv:2406.06608 — comprehensive taxonomy of prompting strategies with empirical comparisons.
